title: Controlled vocabularies
text: common data-tidying problem standardising variant terms concept. Imagine dataset uses number different names shades colour. data analysts, naturally want recode data eliminate messy creativity, example using dplyr::recode(): recoding way can tedious, especially large number terms. control(), can instead use data frame containing thesaurus replace values: default, control() issues message listing values replaced warning values matched thesaurus. can suppressed quiet = TRUE warn_unmatched = FALSE respectively. Use control_names() control names object rather values. useful standardising column names data frames:
code: library(dplyr, warn.conflicts = FALSE) shades <- c("daffodil", "purple", "magenta", "azure", "navy", "violet")  recode(shades,        daffodil = "yellow",        purple = "purple",        magenta = "pink",        azure = "blue",        navy = "blue",        violet = "purple") #> [1] "yellow" "purple" "pink"   "blue"   "blue"   "purple" library(controller) data("colour_thesaurus")  control(shades, colour_thesaurus) #> Replaced values: #> ℹ daffodil → yellow #> ℹ azure → blue #> ℹ navy → blue #> ℹ violet → purple #> Warning: Some values of `x` were not matched in `thesaurus`: #> ✖ magenta #> [1] "yellow"  "purple"  "magenta" "blue"    "blue"    "purple" df <- data.frame(temp = 20, humid = 65, `wind speed` = 10, date = "2024-01-01") df #>   temp humid wind.speed       date #> 1   20    65         10 2024-01-01  control_names(df, thesaurus = data.frame(   preferred = c("temperature", "humidity", "wind_speed"),   variant = c("temp", "humid", "wind speed") )) #> Replaced values: #> ℹ temp → temperature #> ℹ humid → humidity #> Warning: Some values of `x` were not matched in `thesaurus`: #> ✖ wind.speed #> ✖ date #>   temperature humidity wind.speed       date #> 1          20       65         10 2024-01-01

title: Controlled vocabularies
text: control() also supports fuzzy matching, removing need exhaustively list variants common causes differing terminology. example, perform case insensitive match thesaurus: control_fuzzy() goes also ignoring differences word boundaries character encoding. Fuzzy boundary matching treats word boundaries like spaces, hyphens, underscores equivalent: Fuzzy encoding matching ignores non-ASCII characters, plain ASCII input can match variants diacritics wrongly encoded characters (‘mojibake’): inspect type match used value, use control_matches(). returns data frame column match type, rather single vector:
code: shades_ci <- toupper(shades) control_ci(shades_ci, colour_thesaurus) #> Replaced values: #> ℹ DAFFODIL → yellow #> ℹ PURPLE → purple #> ℹ AZURE → blue #> ℹ NAVY → blue #> ℹ VIOLET → purple #> Warning: Some values of `x` were not matched in `thesaurus`: #> ✖ MAGENTA #> [1] "yellow"  "purple"  "MAGENTA" "blue"    "blue"    "purple" df <- data.frame(preferred = "foo bar", variant = "foo-bar") control(c("foo bar", "foo_bar", "foobar"), df,         fuzzy_boundary = TRUE, quiet = FALSE) #> Replaced values: #> ℹ foo_bar → foo bar #> ℹ foobar → foo bar #> [1] "foo bar" "foo bar" "foo bar" df <- data.frame(preferred = "bar", variant = "fo\u00f6") control("foo", df, fuzzy_encoding = TRUE, quiet = FALSE) #> Replaced values: #> ℹ foo → bar #> [1] "bar" control_matches(shades_ci, colour_thesaurus, case_insensitive = TRUE) #>       term exact_match case_insensitive_match #> 1 DAFFODIL        <NA>                 yellow #> 2   PURPLE        <NA>                 purple #> 3  MAGENTA        <NA>                   <NA> #> 4    AZURE        <NA>                   blue #> 5     NAVY        <NA>                   blue #> 6   VIOLET        <NA>                 purple

title: Controlled vocabularies
text: thesaurus data frame two columns containing preferred terms variants controlled. row maps one variant one preferred term. variants must unique values – otherwise unambiguously matched preferred term. colour_thesaurus,1 used examples , example thesaurus bundled package: thesaurus simply data frame, can created read R standard tools: Use thesaurus_cols argument specify columns data frame contain preferred variant terms respectively: package can also read controlled vocabularies Historic England’s FISH (Forum Information Standards Heritage) format using read_fish(). path argument can local .zip file, uncompressed directory, URL: result data frame columns preferred term, ready use control().
code: head(colour_thesaurus) #>   colour     shade #> 1  white     white #> 2  white     pearl #> 3  white alabaster #> 4  white      snow #> 5  white     ivory #> 6  white     cream my_thesaurus <- data.frame(   preferred = c("feline", "canine"),   variant = c("cat", "dog") )  animals <- c("cat", "dog", "parrot") control(animals, my_thesaurus) #> Replaced values: #> ℹ cat → feline #> ℹ dog → canine #> Warning: Some values of `x` were not matched in `thesaurus`: #> ✖ parrot #> [1] "feline" "canine" "parrot" my_wider_thesaurus <- data.frame(   code = c("FEL", "CAN"),   label = c("feline", "canine"),   variant = c("cat", "dog") )  control(animals, my_wider_thesaurus, thesaurus_cols = c("label", "variant")) #> Replaced values: #> ℹ cat → feline #> ℹ dog → canine #> Warning: Some values of `x` were not matched in `thesaurus`: #> ✖ parrot #> [1] "feline" "canine" "parrot" nationality_zip <- system.file("extdata", "fish-nationality.zip",                                package = "controller") fish_nationality <- read_fish(nationality_zip) head(fish_nationality) #>   preferred     term #> 1   FAROESE Faeroese

title: Controlled Vocabularies
text: common data-tidying problem standardising variant terms concept. Imagine dataset uses number different names shades colour. data analysts, naturally want recode data eliminate messy creativity, example using dplyr::recode(): recoding way can tedious, especially large number terms. control(), can instead use data frame containing thesaurus replace values: control() also supports fuzzy matching, removing need exhaustively list variants common causes differing terminology. example, perform case insensitive match thesaurus:
code: library(dplyr, warn.conflicts = FALSE) shades <- c("daffodil", "purple", "magenta", "azure", "navy", "violet")  recode(shades,        daffodil = "yellow",        purple = "purple",        magenta = "pink",        azure = "blue",        navy = "blue",        violet = "purple") #> [1] "yellow" "purple" "pink"   "blue"   "blue"   "purple" library(controller) data("colour_thesaurus")  control(shades, colour_thesaurus) #> Replaced values: #> ℹ daffodil → yellow #> ℹ azure → blue #> ℹ navy → blue #> ℹ violet → purple #> Warning: Some values of `x` were not matched in `thesaurus`: #> ✖ magenta #> [1] "yellow"  "purple"  "magenta" "blue"    "blue"    "purple" shades <- toupper(shades) control_ci(shades, colour_thesaurus) #> Replaced values: #> ℹ DAFFODIL → yellow #> ℹ PURPLE → purple #> ℹ AZURE → blue #> ℹ NAVY → blue #> ℹ VIOLET → purple #> Warning: Some values of `x` were not matched in `thesaurus`: #> ✖ MAGENTA #> [1] "yellow"  "purple"  "MAGENTA" "blue"    "blue"    "purple"

title: Colour thesaurus — colour_thesaurus
text: dataset colour names based Ingrid Sundberg's "colour thesaurus". Contains 240 names shades 12 colours, formatted use thesaurus control().

title: Colour thesaurus — colour_thesaurus
Colour thesaurus — colour_thesaurus
text: data frame 240 rows 2 variables: colour preferred colour name: "white", "tan", "yellow", "orange", "red", "pink", "purple", "blue", "green", "brown", "grey", "black" shade variant names shades colour

title: Colour thesaurus — colour_thesaurus
text: Ingrid Sundberg (https://web.archive.org/web/20250619164626/https://ingridsundberg.com/2014/02/04/-color-thesaurus/)

what: Recode values using a thesaurus — control
title: Recode values using a thesaurus — control
text: control() verb replaces values vector values looked thesaurus. similar switch() dplyr::recode(), replacement values specified data frame instead individual arguments. default control() replaces values x exactly match terms thesaurus. Additional arguments allow case insensitive fuzzy matching strategies (see details). control_ci() control_fuzzy() convenience aliases case insensitive exact matching full fuzzy matching respectively.

title: Recode values using a thesaurus — control
code: control(   x,   thesaurus,   thesaurus_cols = c(1, 2),   case_insensitive = FALSE,   fuzzy_boundary = FALSE,   fuzzy_encoding = FALSE,   quiet = FALSE,   warn_unmatched = TRUE )  control_ci(x, thesaurus, thesaurus_cols = c(1, 2), ...)  control_fuzzy(x, thesaurus, thesaurus_cols = c(1, 2), ...)

title: Recode values using a thesaurus — control
text: x Vector recode. thesaurus Data frame vector preferred terms vector variants. thesaurus_cols Vector two column names positions specifying columns thesaurus contain preferred terms variants respectively. Defaults first two columns. case_insensitive Set TRUE perform case insensitive matching. fuzzy_boundary Set TRUE perform fuzzy matching ignores differences word boundaries used (e.g. "foo bar" matches "foo-bar"). fuzzy_encoding Set TRUE perform fuzzy matching ignores non-ASCII characters may encoded differently (e.g. "foo" matches "foö"). quiet Set TRUE suppress messages replaced values. warn_unmatched TRUE (default), issues warning values matched thesaurus. ... control_ci() control_fuzzy(), arguments passed control(). includes fuzzy matching options (fuzzy_boundary, fuzzy_encoding) output options (quiet, warn_unmatched).

title: Recode values using a thesaurus — control
text: vector length x values matching variants thesaurus replaced preferred term. NAs x preserved NAs. default gives message listing replaced values warning listing values matched thesaurus. can suppressed quiet = TRUE warn_unmatched = FALSE respectively.

title: Recode values using a thesaurus — control
code: data(colour_thesaurus)  # Exact matching x <- c("red", "lipstick", "green", "mint", "blue", "azure") control(x, colour_thesaurus) #> Replaced values: #> ℹ lipstick → red #> ℹ mint → green #> ℹ azure → blue #> [1] "red"   "red"   "green" "green" "blue"  "blue"   # Case insensitive matching x <- toupper(x) control_ci(x, colour_thesaurus) #> Replaced values: #> ℹ RED → red #> ℹ LIPSTICK → red #> ℹ GREEN → green #> ℹ MINT → green #> ℹ BLUE → blue #> ℹ AZURE → blue #> [1] "red"   "red"   "green" "green" "blue"  "blue"   # control_matches() returns a data frame showing which match type was used: control_matches(x, colour_thesaurus, case_insensitive = TRUE) #>       term exact_match case_insensitive_match #> 1      RED        <NA>                    red #> 2 LIPSTICK        <NA>                    red #> 3    GREEN        <NA>                  green #> 4     MINT        <NA>                  green #> 5     BLUE        <NA>                   blue #> 6    AZURE        <NA>                   blue

what: Show detailed match results from a thesaurus — control_matches
title: Show detailed match results from a thesaurus — control_matches
text: control_matches() returns data frame showing type match used value x. useful debugging inspecting control() recodes values.

title: Show detailed match results from a thesaurus — control_matches
code: control_matches(   x,   thesaurus,   thesaurus_cols = c(1, 2),   case_insensitive = FALSE,   fuzzy_boundary = FALSE,   fuzzy_encoding = FALSE )

title: Show detailed match results from a thesaurus — control_matches
text: x Vector recode. thesaurus Data frame vector preferred terms vector variants. thesaurus_cols Vector two column names positions specifying columns thesaurus contain preferred terms variants respectively. Defaults first two columns. case_insensitive Set TRUE perform case insensitive matching. fuzzy_boundary Set TRUE perform fuzzy matching ignores differences word boundaries used (e.g. "foo bar" matches "foo-bar"). fuzzy_encoding Set TRUE perform fuzzy matching ignores non-ASCII characters may encoded differently (e.g. "foo" matches "foö").

title: Show detailed match results from a thesaurus — control_matches
text: data frame number rows x. first column (term) contains original values. Subsequent columns contain match result match type (e.g. exact_match, case_insensitive_match, fuzzy_boundary_match, fuzzy_encoding_match). Rows NA values x NAs.

title: Show detailed match results from a thesaurus — control_matches
code: data(colour_thesaurus)  x <- c("red", "lipstick", "green", "mint", "blue", "azure") control_matches(x, colour_thesaurus) #>       term exact_match #> 1      red         red #> 2 lipstick         red #> 3    green       green #> 4     mint       green #> 5     blue        blue #> 6    azure        blue

what: Control the names of an object — control_names
title: Control the names of an object — control_names
text: control_names() controls names object using thesaurus. extracts names x, passes control(), reassigns result names(x). control_names_ci() control_names_fuzzy() convenience aliases case insensitive full fuzzy matching respectively.

title: Control the names of an object — control_names
code: control_names(x, thesaurus, thesaurus_cols = c(1, 2), ...)  control_names_ci(x, thesaurus, thesaurus_cols = c(1, 2), ...)  control_names_fuzzy(x, thesaurus, thesaurus_cols = c(1, 2), ...)

title: Control the names of an object — control_names
text: x Object names control. thesaurus Data frame vector preferred terms vector variants. thesaurus_cols Vector two column names positions specifying columns thesaurus contain preferred terms variants respectively. Defaults first two columns. ... arguments passed control(). includes fuzzy matching options (case_insensitive, fuzzy_boundary, fuzzy_encoding) output options (quiet, warn_unmatched).

title: Control the names of an object — control_names
text: object x names replaced controlled values.

title: Control the names of an object — control_names
code: df <- data.frame(temp = 20, humid = 65, `wind speed` = 10, date = "2024-01-01") thesaurus <- data.frame(   preferred = c("temperature", "humidity", "wind_speed"),   variant = c("temp", "humid", "wind speed") ) control_names(df, thesaurus) #> Replaced values: #> ℹ temp → temperature #> ℹ humid → humidity #> Warning: Some values of `x` were not matched in `thesaurus`: #> ✖ wind.speed #> ✖ date #>   temperature humidity wind.speed       date #> 1          20       65         10 2024-01-01

title: controller: Controlled Vocabularies — controller-package
text: Work controlled vocabularies; predefined set terms intended used specific context. package introduces 'control' verb, recodes values vector using lookup table preferred variant terms ('thesaurus'). also includes functions reading controlled vocabularies standard formats.

title: controller: Controlled Vocabularies — controller-package
text: Maintainer: Joe Roe joe@joeroe.io (ORCID) [copyright holder] Authors: Joe Roe joe@joeroe.io (ORCID) [copyright holder]

what: Read a FISH controlled vocabulary — read_fish
title: Read a FISH controlled vocabulary — read_fish
text: Reads controlled vocabularies Historic England's FISH (Forum Information Standards Heritage) group. can downloaded : https://heritage-standards.org.uk/fish-vocabularies/.

title: Read a FISH controlled vocabulary — read_fish
Read a FISH controlled vocabulary — read_fish
text: path Path URL vocabulary FISH's CSV format. Can either .zip archive directory containing already uncompressed files, URL.

title: Read a FISH controlled vocabulary — read_fish
text: data frame two columns: preferred term.

title: Read a FISH controlled vocabulary — read_fish
text: Forum Information Standards Heritage, "FISH Thesaurus Table Structure", Available : https://heritage-standards.org.uk/fish-vocabularies/.

title: Read a FISH controlled vocabulary — read_fish
code: # Read a FISH vocabulary from a local zip file nationality_zip <- system.file("extdata", "fish-nationality.zip",                                package = "controller") read_fish(nationality_zip) #>   preferred     term #> 1   FAROESE Faeroese

title: controller 0.0.0.9000
text: Adds control(), control_ci(), control_fuzzy() Adds control_matches() inspecting matches made Adds control_names(), control_names_ci(), control_names_fuzzy() (#7) Adds example dataset colour_thesaurus Adds read_fish() Adds NEWS.md file track changes package.
